content.text: Aristotle's ethical concept of the {{c1::Golden Mean}} (El Justo Medio) states that virtue is found by cultivating character traits that avoid the extremes of {{c1::excess}} and {{c1::deficiency}}.
content.explanation: Virtue is the balance point between two opposing vices. For example, Courage is the Golden Mean between Cowardice (deficiency of confidence) and Rashness (excess of confidence).
content.spanish: El concepto ético de Aristóteles del Justo Medio establece que la virtud se encuentra al cultivar rasgos de carácter que evitan los extremos del exceso y la deficiencia.
content.usage: To practice the Golden Mean, analyze your behaviors: do not act out of fear (deficiency) or act recklessly without thinking (excess), but act assertively and deliberately (mean).

content.text: The Socratic Method of cooperative argumentative dialogue is primarily used to stimulate critical thinking and expose contradictions in assumptions through {{c1::methodical questioning (Elenchus)}}.
content.explanation: Socrates did not lecture; instead, he asked questions that forced his interlocutors to clarify their definitions and recognize the logical limits or contradictions in their beliefs.
content.spanish: El método socrático de diálogo argumentativo cooperativo se utiliza principalmente para estimular el pensamiento crítico y exponer contradicciones en los supuestos mediante preguntas metódicas (Elenchus).
content.usage:
Example Question: What is justice?
  • The interlocutor provides a definition.
  • Socrates asks a counter-example question.
  • The interlocutor revises the definition.

content.scenario: Philosophy: Plato's Allegory of the Cave 🏛️
content.text: Plato's Allegory of the Cave illustrates that humans mistake sensory appearances for reality, and that true knowledge is obtained only by escaping the cave to perceive the {{c1::Theory of Forms (Mundo de las Ideas)}}.
content.explanation: The cave dwellers see only shadows cast on walls by a fire and believe the shadows are real objects. Escaping the cave represents the philosopher's journey of using reason to see the immutable, true essence (Forms) of reality.
content.spanish: La alegoría de la cueva de Platón ilustra que los humanos confunden las apariencias sensoriales con la realidad, y que el conocimiento verdadero se obtiene únicamente al escapar de la cueva para percibir la Teoría de las Formas.
content.usage: Core teaching: The physical world is a shadow/copy; the world of Ideas is the ultimate, immutable truth.

content.text: Early Greek philosophers sought the fundamental, originating substance of the cosmos, which they called the {{c1::Arche}}.
content.explanation: The Arche represents the primary material or source of all things. For Thales, the Arche was water; for Anaximenes, it was air; for Heraclitus, it was fire.
content.spanish: Los primeros filósofos griegos buscaron la sustancia fundamental y originaria del cosmos, a la que llamaron el Arjé (Arche).
content.usage: The search for the Arche marks the birth of rational metaphysics, moving away from mythological explanations.

content.text: Epicurus taught that the greatest good is to seek pleasure and avoid pain to achieve a state of mental tranquility known as {{c1::Ataraxia}}.
content.explanation: Ataraxia is a state of serene calmness and freedom from fear and worry, achieved by understanding the natural limits of desires and overcoming the fear of death.
content.spanish: Epicuro enseñó que el mayor bien es buscar el placer y evitar el dolor para lograr un estado de tranquilidad mental conocido como ataraxia.
content.usage: Ataraxia is contrasted with active, physical pleasure; it is the pleasure of absence of pain (aponia) and anxiety.

content.text: The philosophical school of {{c1::Cynicism}}, led by Diogenes of Sinope, advocated living in extreme simplicity and accordance with nature, rejecting social conventions.
content.explanation: Cynics believed that virtue is the only good, and that happiness is achieved by being self-sufficient and living naturally, free from desires for wealth, power, or fame.
content.spanish: La escuela filosófica del cinismo (Cynicism), liderada por Diógenes de Sinope, abogaba por vivir en extrema simplicidad y de acuerdo con la naturaleza, rechazando las convenciones sociales.
content.usage: Diogenes famously lived in a tub on the streets of Athens, mocking social status and conventions.

content.text: Skeptics argue that absolute knowledge is impossible, and that one should practice suspension of judgment, known as {{c1::Epoche}}.
content.explanation: Epoche is the suspension of belief or judgment regarding non-evident assertions. Pyrrhonian skeptics believed that by suspending judgment, one achieves tranquility (ataraxia) by stopping the search for dogmatic truths.
content.spanish: Los escépticos argumentan que el conocimiento absoluto es imposible, y que uno debería practicar la suspensión del juicio, conocida como epojé (Epoche).
content.usage: Epoche is a method of questioning assumptions to prevent dogmatism.

content.text: To construct logical deductive arguments, Aristotle developed the {{c1::syllogism}}, consisting of a major premise, minor premise, and a conclusion.
content.explanation: A syllogism is a form of reasoning where a conclusion is drawn from two assumed propositions. Example: 'All humans are mortal (major); Socrates is human (minor); therefore, Socrates is mortal (conclusion).'
content.spanish: Para construir argumentos deductivos lógicos, Aristóteles desarrolló el silogismo (syllogism), que consta de una premisa mayor, una premisa menor y una conclusión.
content.usage: Syllogistic logic remained the dominant framework of formal logic in the West until the late 19th century.

content.text: In the Republic, Plato proposes that the ideal state should be ruled by {{c1::philosopher kings}} who possess knowledge of the Good.
content.explanation: Plato argued that because philosophers have access to the immutable world of Forms (truth and justice), they are uniquely qualified to rule without greed or corruption, guiding society toward harmony.
content.spanish: En la República, Platón propone que el estado ideal debería ser gobernado por reyes filósofos (philosopher kings) que poseen el conocimiento del Bien.
content.usage: Plato's philosopher kings represent the rule of reason and intellect over physical desires and military power.

content.text: Socrates' ethical thesis that no one does evil knowingly, and that doing wrong is always a result of ignorance, is known as {{c1::virtue is knowledge}}.
content.explanation: This is Socratic intellectualism. Socrates believed that if a person truly understands what is good and just, they will naturally act virtuously. Acting maliciously is therefore a cognitive failure (ignorance).
content.spanish: La tesis ética de Sócrates de que nadie hace el mal a sabiendas, y que actuar mal es siempre resultado de la ignorancia, se conoce como la virtud es conocimiento (virtue is knowledge).
content.usage: Under this view, rehabilitation and philosophical dialogue are preferred over retributive punishment.

content.text: The core Taoist concept of {{c1::Wu Wei}} (non-action) refers to a state of alignment with the flow of life, acting effortlessly without {{c1::force or struggle}}.
content.explanation: Wu Wei does not mean laziness; it means acting in a way that is natural and fluid, like water flowing around obstacles, rather than fighting against them.
content.spanish: El concepto taoísta central de Wu Wei (no acción) se refiere a un estado de alineación con el flujo de la vida, actuando sin esfuerzo ni fuerza.
content.usage: Instead of forcing a conversation or trying to control a situation that isn't working, practice Wu Wei by stepping back and letting events develop naturally.

content.text: In Taoism, the concept of effortless action or acting in alignment with the natural flow of the universe is known as {{c1::Wu Wei}}.
content.explanation: Wu Wei (non-action or effortless action) does not mean laziness; it means acting without forced struggle, adapting to situations organically, much like water flowing around an obstacle.
content.spanish: En el taoísmo, el concepto de acción sin esfuerzo o actuar en alineación con el flujo natural del universo se conoce como Wu Wei.
content.usage: To practice Wu Wei, avoid forcing outcomes and let events take their natural course.

content.text: In Buddhism, the doctrine that all conditioned things are in a constant state of change and flux is called {{c1::Anicca}} (Impermanence).
content.explanation: Anicca is one of the Three Marks of Existence in Buddhism. It asserts that nothing in the physical or mental world is static or permanent, and attachment to transient things is the source of suffering.
content.spanish: En el budismo, la doctrina de que todas las cosas condicionadas están en un estado constante de cambio y flujo se llama Anicca (impermanencia).
content.usage: Reflecting on Anicca helps practitioners cultivate non-attachment and accept life's changes.

content.text: The Buddhist concept that there is no permanent, unchanging soul or self in living beings is known as {{c1::Anatta}} (Non-Self).
content.explanation: Anatta asserts that what we call the 'self' is a temporary bundle of physical and mental aggregates (Skandhas). Realizing that there is no independent 'I' is key to achieving Nirvana.
content.spanish: El concepto budista de que no existe un alma o yo permanente e inmutable en los seres vivos se conoce como Anatta (inexistencia del yo).
content.usage: Anatta challenges the conventional idea of a fixed identity or soul.

content.text: In Confucianism, the core virtue of respect for one's parents, ancestors, and elders is known as {{c1::Xiao}} (Filial Piety).
content.explanation: Xiao is the foundation of social harmony in Confucian thought. It establishes the moral duties of children toward parents, forming the basis of all hierarchical societal relationships.
content.spanish: En el confucianismo, la virtud fundamental del respeto a los padres, ancestros y mayores se conoce como Xiao (piedad filial).
content.usage: Filial piety requires not just supporting parents but honoring and respecting them.

content.text: Confucius taught that the ultimate moral quality of human-heartedness, empathy, and benevolence toward others is {{c1::Ren}}.
content.explanation: Ren (humaneness or benevolence) is the highest Confucian virtue. It represents the ethical duty to treat others with empathy and respect, maintaining social cohesion.
content.spanish: Confucio enseñó que la cualidad moral máxima de empatía, humanidad y benevolencia hacia los demás es el Ren.
content.usage: Ren is often expressed through the golden rule: 'Do not do to others what you do not want done to yourself.'

content.text: In Hinduism, the moral law, cosmic order, and personal duty one must follow in accordance with their stage of life and caste is {{c1::Dharma}}.
content.explanation: Dharma represents the ethical duties that sustain social and cosmic order. Acting in accordance with Dharma generates good karma, leading to spiritual progress.
content.spanish: En el hinduismo, la ley moral, el orden cósmico y el deber personal que uno debe seguir de acuerdo con su etapa de vida y casta es el Dharma.
content.usage: In the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna urges Arjuna to follow his Dharma as a warrior.

content.text: The ultimate goal in Hinduism, representing liberation from the cycle of rebirth (Samsara) and union with the ultimate reality, is {{c1::Moksha}}.
content.explanation: Moksha is the release of the soul (Atman) from the material world and its union with Brahman. It is achieved through knowledge, devotion, and selfless action.
content.spanish: La meta última en el hinduismo, que representa la liberación del ciclo de reencarnación (Samsara) y la unión con la realidad absoluta, es Moksha.
content.usage: Moksha marks the end of karmic accumulation and the cycle of reincarnation.

content.text: The first Noble Truth of Buddhism states that life is characterized by dissatisfaction, pain, and suffering, a concept known as {{c1::Dukkha}}.
content.explanation: Dukkha refers to the inherent unsatisfactoriness of mundane life. It arises from craving (Tanha) and attachment to impermanent things.
content.spanish: La primera Noble Verdad del budismo establece que la vida se caracteriza por la insatisfacción, el dolor y el sufrimiento, un concepto conocido como Dukkha.
content.usage: Understanding Dukkha is the first step toward walking the Eightfold Path to eliminate suffering.

content.text: The undefinable, absolute principle that is the source, pattern, and substance of everything that exists in Taoism is called the {{c1::Tao}} (The Way).
content.explanation: The Tao is the cosmic flow that governs the universe. It cannot be fully described in words; instead, it is experienced by living in harmony and simplicity.
content.spanish: El principio absoluto e indefinible que es la fuente, el patrón y la sustancia de todo lo que existe en el taoísmo se llama el Tao (El Camino).
content.usage: Opening line of the Tao Te Ching: 'The Tao that can be spoken of is not the eternal Tao.'
